narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 40, which stands out above the typical student's score of around 37. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated your risk of failure at 60%—a threshold that led to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key elements in your background that tipped the scales toward higher risk. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, lower than the average of 3.21, was the strongest factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure, suggesting it sees this as a notable hurdle compared to most applicants. Similarly, your third-year school decile ranking of 3, below the typical 5.5, reinforced that concern, indicating performance that lagged behind many peers. Your middle family income bracket also contributed somewhat to the risk assessment, as it aligns with patterns the model associates with added challenges. On a brighter note, your LSAT score had the most significant positive pull, strongly nudging the prediction toward success and showing your potential in that area—far better than average. Your first-year decile of 3 and full-time enrollment status played smaller roles, both slightly increasing the predicted risk but not as dominantly. Overall, while your LSAT shines, the model weighed your GPA, later school rankings, and income as bigger red flags when compared to typical students who pass the bar. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential—it's just how these factors stacked up statistically. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your GPA and decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT-level prep for other areas, or exploring post-baccalaureate options to strengthen your academic record before reapplying. You're capable of turning this around, and many in similar situations have succeeded with focused improvements—keep pushing.
